preFix.title: Fish finger sandwich
preFix.subtitle: Oven-baked fish fingers in white bread with tartare sauce or ketchup
preFix.excerpt: The fish finger sandwich is a British childhood institution that adults quietly continue to eat. Oven-baked fish fingers in soft white bread with tartare sauce and a little shredded lettuce is simple and satisfying, and takes less than 25 minutes.
preFix.sourceNotes: Birds Eye fish fingers were launched in the UK in 1955 and quickly became a British household staple. The fish finger sandwich became a popular British lunch and comfort food from the 1960s onward, eaten across all demographics.

content.text: The fish finger sandwich relies on good oven timing and the right bread. The fish fingers need to be properly crisp, not pale, and the bread should be soft white. Tartare sauce is the better choice over ketchup here, though the point is not up for serious debate. A little shredded iceberg or a few leaves of lettuce gives it some freshness.

Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Place the fish fingers on a baking tray in a single layer. Bake for 18 to 22 minutes, turning once halfway, until golden and crisp on both sides. Follow the packet cooking time if different.
Butter both slices of bread. Spread tartare sauce (or ketchup) on one side. Add the shredded lettuce if using.
Lay five hot fish fingers on the base of each sandwich. Close the sandwich, press gently, and serve immediately.

items.fix: Use a high oven temperature and turn halfway through. They should be deep golden, not pale cream.
items.cause: Temperature too low, or they were not turned during cooking.
items.symptom: The fish fingers are pale and soft

items.fix: Five fingers per sandwich works for a standard thick slice. Stack them in two rows of two with one on top.
items.cause: Too many fish fingers for the bread size, or too much sauce.

items.fix: Build the sandwich just as the fish fingers come out of the oven, and eat straight away.
items.cause: Assembled and left too long before eating.
items.symptom: The fish fingers steam inside the sandwich and lose their crunch

content.text: Fish fingers have been in British freezers since 1955. The fish finger sandwich has been eaten by children, students, and adults ever since, with no apology. It started as children's food and never stopped. Heston Blumenthal once named it one of his favourite meals, which it neither needed nor cared about.
